channel.title: Best Mortgages For You – UK Property & Mortgage News
channel.description: Latest UK property and mortgage news from leading industry blogs.

item.title: A fool proof stamp duty savings scheme… hidden in plain sight
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Stamp Duty Land Tax (SDLT) remains one of Britain's most contentious property taxes, affecting everyone from first-time buyers to international investors purchasing multimillion-pound mansions. With rates reaching as high as 19% for some overseas buyers, homeowners may wish to explore legitimate ways to mitigate these costs whilst remaining fully compliant with HMRC rules.

Understanding the true cost of SDLT

Since the 2014 reforms, SDLT has become increasingly complex, with additional surcharges for second homes, buy-to-let properties and non-UK residents. The current structure means:

  • Non-residents purchasing a £20m property could face a £3.8m SDLT bill
  • Additional 2% surcharge for non-UK buyers on top of standard rates
  • Higher rates applying to entire purchase price bands, not just amounts above thresholds

These substantial upfront costs come before considering ongoing maintenance expenses for high-value properties, which can easily exceed £500,000 annually for large estates with extensive facilities.

Potential strategies to consider

Whilst SDLT avoidance is illegal, there are legitimate planning opportunities that could reduce liabilities:

  • Exploring mixed-use property classifications where commercial elements may qualify for lower rates
  • Considering phased purchase structures for development projects
  • Reviewing potential reliefs such as multiple dwellings relief where applicable
  • Timing purchases to align with temporary SDLT holidays or threshold changes

Property investors might also weigh SDLT costs against other factors like location, potential rental yields and long-term capital growth prospects when making purchasing decisions.

With careful planning and professional advice, buyers could potentially navigate the SDLT landscape more efficiently. However, it remains crucial to ensure all arrangements comply fully with HMRC regulations to avoid penalties or investigations.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Stamp Duty Land Tax (SDLT) remains one of Britain's most contentious property taxes, affecting everyone from first-time buyers to international investors purchasing multimillion-pound mansions. With rates reaching as high as 19% for some overseas buyers, homeowners may wish to explore legitimate ways to mitigate these costs whilst remaining fully compliant with HMRC rules.

Understanding the true cost of SDLT

Since the 2014 reforms, SDLT has become increasingly complex, with additional surcharges for second homes, buy-to-let properties and non-UK residents. The current structure means:

  • Non-residents purchasing a £20m property could face a £3.8m SDLT bill
  • Additional 2% surcharge for non-UK buyers on top of standard rates
  • Higher rates applying to entire purchase price bands, not just amounts above thresholds

These substantial upfront costs come before considering ongoing maintenance expenses for high-value properties, which can easily exceed £500,000 annually for large estates with extensive facilities.

Potential strategies to consider

Whilst SDLT avoidance is illegal, there are legitimate planning opportunities that could reduce liabilities:

  • Exploring mixed-use property classifications where commercial elements may qualify for lower rates
  • Considering phased purchase structures for development projects
  • Reviewing potential reliefs such as multiple dwellings relief where applicable
  • Timing purchases to align with temporary SDLT holidays or threshold changes

Property investors might also weigh SDLT costs against other factors like location, potential rental yields and long-term capital growth prospects when making purchasing decisions.

With careful planning and professional advice, buyers could potentially navigate the SDLT landscape more efficiently. However, it remains crucial to ensure all arrangements comply fully with HMRC regulations to avoid penalties or investigations.

item.title: Main cause of tenancy deposit disputes revealed
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Cleaning remains the most frequent cause of disputes between landlords and tenants when it comes to tenancy deposits, according to the latest figures from The Deposit Protection Service (DPS). In 2025, cleaning-related issues accounted for nearly 30% of all deposit claims, marking the fifth consecutive year it has topped the list of disagreements. Damage to property and rent arrears followed, making up 18.42% and 16.45% of claims respectively.

Why Cleaning Causes the Most Disputes

Cleaning has consistently been the primary source of contention at the end of a tenancy. Landlords often expect properties to be returned in a spotless condition, whilst tenants may feel they have left the property sufficiently clean. This mismatch in expectations can lead to disputes over deductions from the deposit. Under tenancy deposit rules, landlords are entitled to deduct reasonable costs to cover any cleaning expenses incurred after a tenant moves out.

Matt Trevett, Managing Director of The DPS, emphasised the importance of clear communication between landlords and tenants. He noted that understanding obligations at the end of a tenancy can help reduce disagreements. “Less than 5% of all deposits protected by The DPS end in a dispute,” he added, highlighting the impartial dispute resolution service available to both parties when an agreement cannot be reached.

Other Common Causes of Deposit Disputes

Whilst cleaning dominates the list, damage to property and rent arrears are also significant contributors to deposit disputes. Damage claims often arise from wear and tear beyond what is considered reasonable, whilst rent arrears disputes occur when tenants fail to pay their rent in full before vacating the property.

Interestingly, disputes related to redecoration have seen a steady increase over the past five years, rising by 3.4 percentage points since 2021. This suggests that landlords and tenants may have differing views on what constitutes acceptable alterations or changes to a property’s appearance during a tenancy.

How to Avoid Deposit Disputes

Tenants may wish to take proactive steps to minimise the risk of deposit disputes. Ensuring the property is left in a clean and undamaged condition, as per the tenancy agreement, can help avoid disagreements. It could also be worth documenting the property’s condition at both the start and end of the tenancy through photographs or videos.

On the other hand, landlords might consider providing tenants with a clear checklist of their expectations before the tenancy ends. Open communication can go a long way in preventing misunderstandings and ensuring a smoother transition for both parties.

Whilst disputes are relatively rare, understanding the common causes and taking preventative measures can help landlords and tenants navigate the end of a tenancy more harmoniously.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Cleaning remains the most frequent cause of disputes between landlords and tenants when it comes to tenancy deposits, according to the latest figures from The Deposit Protection Service (DPS). In 2025, cleaning-related issues accounted for nearly 30% of all deposit claims, marking the fifth consecutive year it has topped the list of disagreements. Damage to property and rent arrears followed, making up 18.42% and 16.45% of claims respectively.

Why Cleaning Causes the Most Disputes

Cleaning has consistently been the primary source of contention at the end of a tenancy. Landlords often expect properties to be returned in a spotless condition, whilst tenants may feel they have left the property sufficiently clean. This mismatch in expectations can lead to disputes over deductions from the deposit. Under tenancy deposit rules, landlords are entitled to deduct reasonable costs to cover any cleaning expenses incurred after a tenant moves out.

Matt Trevett, Managing Director of The DPS, emphasised the importance of clear communication between landlords and tenants. He noted that understanding obligations at the end of a tenancy can help reduce disagreements. “Less than 5% of all deposits protected by The DPS end in a dispute,” he added, highlighting the impartial dispute resolution service available to both parties when an agreement cannot be reached.

Other Common Causes of Deposit Disputes

Whilst cleaning dominates the list, damage to property and rent arrears are also significant contributors to deposit disputes. Damage claims often arise from wear and tear beyond what is considered reasonable, whilst rent arrears disputes occur when tenants fail to pay their rent in full before vacating the property.

Interestingly, disputes related to redecoration have seen a steady increase over the past five years, rising by 3.4 percentage points since 2021. This suggests that landlords and tenants may have differing views on what constitutes acceptable alterations or changes to a property’s appearance during a tenancy.

How to Avoid Deposit Disputes

Tenants may wish to take proactive steps to minimise the risk of deposit disputes. Ensuring the property is left in a clean and undamaged condition, as per the tenancy agreement, can help avoid disagreements. It could also be worth documenting the property’s condition at both the start and end of the tenancy through photographs or videos.

On the other hand, landlords might consider providing tenants with a clear checklist of their expectations before the tenancy ends. Open communication can go a long way in preventing misunderstandings and ensuring a smoother transition for both parties.

Whilst disputes are relatively rare, understanding the common causes and taking preventative measures can help landlords and tenants navigate the end of a tenancy more harmoniously.

item.title: Biggest June house price drop in 14 years
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

New data from Rightmove has revealed the largest June house price drop in 14 years, as an increase in property supply and softer demand from buyers shifts the balance of negotiating power. This significant dip highlights changing dynamics in the UK housing market, offering potential opportunities for those looking to move or invest.

What’s Driving the Drop?

Several factors have contributed to this notable decline in house prices. Firstly, the supply of available properties has risen, giving buyers more options to choose from. Additionally, demand has softened, partly due to higher mortgage rates and broader economic uncertainty. These conditions have created a more buyer-friendly market, where negotiation leverage has shifted in favour of purchasers.

This trend marks a departure from the intense competition seen in recent years, where limited stock often led to bidding wars and rapid price growth. With more homes on the market and fewer buyers actively searching, sellers may need to adjust their expectations to secure a sale.

Implications for Buyers and Sellers

For prospective buyers, this shift could present an opportunity to secure a property at a more favourable price. With increased negotiating power, buyers might find it easier to agree on a deal that aligns with their budget. However, it’s worth noting that mortgage rates remain higher than in previous years, so affordability should be carefully assessed.

On the other hand, sellers may need to adopt a more realistic approach to pricing. While the market remains active, properties priced competitively are more likely to attract interest. Homeowners considering selling might benefit from seeking professional advice to ensure their pricing strategy reflects current market conditions.

The UK housing market continues to evolve, with this June’s price drop highlighting the importance of staying informed. Whether buying or selling, understanding these trends can help individuals make more informed decisions in a changing landscape.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

New data from Rightmove has revealed the largest June house price drop in 14 years, as an increase in property supply and softer demand from buyers shifts the balance of negotiating power. This significant dip highlights changing dynamics in the UK housing market, offering potential opportunities for those looking to move or invest.

What’s Driving the Drop?

Several factors have contributed to this notable decline in house prices. Firstly, the supply of available properties has risen, giving buyers more options to choose from. Additionally, demand has softened, partly due to higher mortgage rates and broader economic uncertainty. These conditions have created a more buyer-friendly market, where negotiation leverage has shifted in favour of purchasers.

This trend marks a departure from the intense competition seen in recent years, where limited stock often led to bidding wars and rapid price growth. With more homes on the market and fewer buyers actively searching, sellers may need to adjust their expectations to secure a sale.

Implications for Buyers and Sellers

For prospective buyers, this shift could present an opportunity to secure a property at a more favourable price. With increased negotiating power, buyers might find it easier to agree on a deal that aligns with their budget. However, it’s worth noting that mortgage rates remain higher than in previous years, so affordability should be carefully assessed.

On the other hand, sellers may need to adopt a more realistic approach to pricing. While the market remains active, properties priced competitively are more likely to attract interest. Homeowners considering selling might benefit from seeking professional advice to ensure their pricing strategy reflects current market conditions.

The UK housing market continues to evolve, with this June’s price drop highlighting the importance of staying informed. Whether buying or selling, understanding these trends can help individuals make more informed decisions in a changing landscape.

item.title: Residential property no longer reliable retirement investment
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Recent analysis has cast doubt on residential property's traditional position as a dependable retirement investment, with data showing it has delivered weaker returns than both equities and inflation over comparable periods.

Property underperformance raises questions

Whilst bricks and mortar have long been considered a cornerstone of retirement planning, fresh research indicates UK residential property has struggled to keep pace with alternative investments. The findings suggest homeowners relying solely on property wealth might need to reconsider their long-term financial strategies.

Key factors contributing to this trend include:

  • Slower house price growth in many regions compared to historical averages
  • Increasing maintenance costs and taxation changes affecting net returns
  • Stock market investments outperforming property over multiple timeframes

Diversification considerations for retirement planning

Financial experts increasingly emphasise the importance of diversified portfolios rather than over-reliance on any single asset class. Those approaching retirement might wish to explore:

  • Spreading investments across different asset types
  • Considering pension products alongside property ownership
  • Reviewing inheritance tax implications of different investment approaches

The research doesn't suggest property has lost all value as an investment, but rather highlights the potential risks of depending on it exclusively for retirement income. As with all financial decisions, seeking professional advice tailored to individual circumstances remains crucial.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Recent analysis has cast doubt on residential property's traditional position as a dependable retirement investment, with data showing it has delivered weaker returns than both equities and inflation over comparable periods.

Property underperformance raises questions

Whilst bricks and mortar have long been considered a cornerstone of retirement planning, fresh research indicates UK residential property has struggled to keep pace with alternative investments. The findings suggest homeowners relying solely on property wealth might need to reconsider their long-term financial strategies.

Key factors contributing to this trend include:

  • Slower house price growth in many regions compared to historical averages
  • Increasing maintenance costs and taxation changes affecting net returns
  • Stock market investments outperforming property over multiple timeframes

Diversification considerations for retirement planning

Financial experts increasingly emphasise the importance of diversified portfolios rather than over-reliance on any single asset class. Those approaching retirement might wish to explore:

  • Spreading investments across different asset types
  • Considering pension products alongside property ownership
  • Reviewing inheritance tax implications of different investment approaches

The research doesn't suggest property has lost all value as an investment, but rather highlights the potential risks of depending on it exclusively for retirement income. As with all financial decisions, seeking professional advice tailored to individual circumstances remains crucial.

item.title: Birmingham becoming property investment hotspot
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Why Birmingham's property market is drawing investor attention

Birmingham's property market has emerged as a compelling proposition for investors, with a combination of strong capital appreciation, healthy rental yields and transformative regeneration schemes reshaping the city's landscape. The Midlands metropolis now presents some intriguing opportunities for those considering buy-to-let investments or property portfolio expansion.

Key factors driving Birmingham's appeal

Several elements are contributing to Birmingham's growing status as an investment destination:

  • Price growth potential: While more affordable than London, Birmingham has shown consistent house price increases that outperform many regional cities
  • Rental demand: A growing population of young professionals and students continues to fuel strong tenant demand across central areas
  • Infrastructure projects: Major developments like HS2 and the ongoing city centre regeneration are enhancing Birmingham's long-term prospects
  • Regeneration zones: Areas such as Digbeth and Smithfield are undergoing significant transformation, creating new investment hotspots

Considerations for potential investors

Whilst Birmingham's market shows promise, homeowners may wish to carefully evaluate several factors before committing:

  • The varying performance between different neighbourhoods and property types
  • Local rental market dynamics and potential yields
  • Ongoing maintenance costs and management requirements
  • Financing options and mortgage product availability for investment properties

With its combination of relative affordability and growth potential, Birmingham's property market continues to attract attention from investors seeking alternatives to London's premium prices. As with any investment decision, thorough research and professional financial advice remain essential.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Why Birmingham's property market is drawing investor attention

Birmingham's property market has emerged as a compelling proposition for investors, with a combination of strong capital appreciation, healthy rental yields and transformative regeneration schemes reshaping the city's landscape. The Midlands metropolis now presents some intriguing opportunities for those considering buy-to-let investments or property portfolio expansion.

Key factors driving Birmingham's appeal

Several elements are contributing to Birmingham's growing status as an investment destination:

  • Price growth potential: While more affordable than London, Birmingham has shown consistent house price increases that outperform many regional cities
  • Rental demand: A growing population of young professionals and students continues to fuel strong tenant demand across central areas
  • Infrastructure projects: Major developments like HS2 and the ongoing city centre regeneration are enhancing Birmingham's long-term prospects
  • Regeneration zones: Areas such as Digbeth and Smithfield are undergoing significant transformation, creating new investment hotspots

Considerations for potential investors

Whilst Birmingham's market shows promise, homeowners may wish to carefully evaluate several factors before committing:

  • The varying performance between different neighbourhoods and property types
  • Local rental market dynamics and potential yields
  • Ongoing maintenance costs and management requirements
  • Financing options and mortgage product availability for investment properties

With its combination of relative affordability and growth potential, Birmingham's property market continues to attract attention from investors seeking alternatives to London's premium prices. As with any investment decision, thorough research and professional financial advice remain essential.

item.title: Open Banking for Real Estate Firms: How UK Property Operators Can Improve Tenant Checks, Rent Collection and Payouts
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

In the UK property sector, many letting agencies, property managers, and portfolio operators still rely on outdated methods for tenant checks, rent collection, and contractor payments. Emailed payslips, manual reconciliations, and individual bank transfers remain commonplace. However, the introduction of Open Banking could revolutionise these processes, offering a more streamlined and efficient approach.

What is Open Banking?

Open Banking is a regulatory initiative that allows third-party financial service providers to access banking information securely, with the customer’s consent. Introduced in 2018, it aims to foster innovation, competition, and transparency in the financial sector. For property operators, this technology can simplify and automate many routine tasks, reducing administrative burdens and improving accuracy.

Benefits for Tenant Checks

Tenant referencing is a critical part of the letting process, but it often involves time-consuming steps like requesting and verifying payslips or bank statements. Open Banking can streamline this process by providing instant access to verified financial data. This not only speeds up tenant checks but also reduces the risk of fraudulent documentation. Property managers could assess affordability more accurately, ensuring tenants are well-suited to the property.

Improving Rent Collection

Rent collection is another area where Open Banking could make a significant impact. Currently, many agencies rely on direct debits or card payments, which can lead to delays and reconciliation issues. With Open Banking, rent payments could be automated and tracked in real-time, reducing the need for manual intervention. This could minimise late payments and improve cash flow for landlords and property operators.

Simplifying Contractor Payments

Paying contractors often involves processing multiple invoices and making individual bank transfers, which can be both time-consuming and prone to errors. Open Banking could simplify this process by enabling bulk payments and automating reconciliation. This would save time and ensure contractors are paid promptly, fostering better relationships and improving operational efficiency.

Open Banking has the potential to transform the UK property sector by modernising traditional processes. From tenant checks to rent collection and contractor payments, this technology offers a more efficient and transparent way of managing operations. Property operators and letting agencies might consider exploring Open Banking solutions to stay competitive and enhance their service offerings.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

In the UK property sector, many letting agencies, property managers, and portfolio operators still rely on outdated methods for tenant checks, rent collection, and contractor payments. Emailed payslips, manual reconciliations, and individual bank transfers remain commonplace. However, the introduction of Open Banking could revolutionise these processes, offering a more streamlined and efficient approach.

What is Open Banking?

Open Banking is a regulatory initiative that allows third-party financial service providers to access banking information securely, with the customer’s consent. Introduced in 2018, it aims to foster innovation, competition, and transparency in the financial sector. For property operators, this technology can simplify and automate many routine tasks, reducing administrative burdens and improving accuracy.

Benefits for Tenant Checks

Tenant referencing is a critical part of the letting process, but it often involves time-consuming steps like requesting and verifying payslips or bank statements. Open Banking can streamline this process by providing instant access to verified financial data. This not only speeds up tenant checks but also reduces the risk of fraudulent documentation. Property managers could assess affordability more accurately, ensuring tenants are well-suited to the property.

Improving Rent Collection

Rent collection is another area where Open Banking could make a significant impact. Currently, many agencies rely on direct debits or card payments, which can lead to delays and reconciliation issues. With Open Banking, rent payments could be automated and tracked in real-time, reducing the need for manual intervention. This could minimise late payments and improve cash flow for landlords and property operators.

Simplifying Contractor Payments

Paying contractors often involves processing multiple invoices and making individual bank transfers, which can be both time-consuming and prone to errors. Open Banking could simplify this process by enabling bulk payments and automating reconciliation. This would save time and ensure contractors are paid promptly, fostering better relationships and improving operational efficiency.

Open Banking has the potential to transform the UK property sector by modernising traditional processes. From tenant checks to rent collection and contractor payments, this technology offers a more efficient and transparent way of managing operations. Property operators and letting agencies might consider exploring Open Banking solutions to stay competitive and enhance their service offerings.

item.title: Almost 2,000 agents gather for Propertymark One
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description: 关于这个问题，我没有相关信息，您可以尝试问我其它问题，我会尽力为您解答~
item.content:encoded: 关于这个问题，我没有相关信息，您可以尝试问我其它问题，我会尽力为您解答~

item.title: Fake landlord jailed after £77,000 rental scam
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

A London man has been sentenced to nearly three years in prison after defrauding prospective tenants out of over £77,000 in a rental scam involving a property he did not legitimately let. Freddie Priestley, 34, from Southwark, received a 35-month jail term at Inner London Crown Court after admitting multiple counts of fraud by false representation.

How the rental scam operated

The court heard how Priestley advertised a spare room in his Bermondsey flat to multiple tenants, accepting deposits and initial rent payments. He then fabricated various excuses to prevent them from moving in, while keeping their money. Prosecutors revealed that in some instances, funds taken from new victims were used to partially repay earlier ones – a pattern characteristic of fraudulent schemes.

Authorities identified at least £77,406 in fraudulent transactions, with many tenants losing significant sums. The judge noted the substantial impact on victims, some of whom provided impact statements detailing financial hardship and housing instability caused by the scam.

Sentencing and mitigating factors

During proceedings, Priestley's defence team cited ADHD and a gambling addiction as contributing factors. His lawyer stated that all funds – including legitimate earnings – had been lost to gambling, leaving him with no assets. However, the court determined these circumstances didn't justify the sustained fraudulent activity.

In delivering the sentence, the judge emphasised the severity of the offences: "The consequences for victims were so detrimental that only an immediate custodial sentence would be appropriate." The 35-month term reflects both the financial scale of the fraud and its impact on multiple vulnerable renters.

This case serves as a stark reminder for tenants to verify landlord credentials and rental agreements thoroughly. Whilst most landlords operate legitimately, prospective renters might consider checking property ownership details and using protected deposit schemes to reduce fraud risks.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

A London man has been sentenced to nearly three years in prison after defrauding prospective tenants out of over £77,000 in a rental scam involving a property he did not legitimately let. Freddie Priestley, 34, from Southwark, received a 35-month jail term at Inner London Crown Court after admitting multiple counts of fraud by false representation.

How the rental scam operated

The court heard how Priestley advertised a spare room in his Bermondsey flat to multiple tenants, accepting deposits and initial rent payments. He then fabricated various excuses to prevent them from moving in, while keeping their money. Prosecutors revealed that in some instances, funds taken from new victims were used to partially repay earlier ones – a pattern characteristic of fraudulent schemes.

Authorities identified at least £77,406 in fraudulent transactions, with many tenants losing significant sums. The judge noted the substantial impact on victims, some of whom provided impact statements detailing financial hardship and housing instability caused by the scam.

Sentencing and mitigating factors

During proceedings, Priestley's defence team cited ADHD and a gambling addiction as contributing factors. His lawyer stated that all funds – including legitimate earnings – had been lost to gambling, leaving him with no assets. However, the court determined these circumstances didn't justify the sustained fraudulent activity.

In delivering the sentence, the judge emphasised the severity of the offences: "The consequences for victims were so detrimental that only an immediate custodial sentence would be appropriate." The 35-month term reflects both the financial scale of the fraud and its impact on multiple vulnerable renters.

This case serves as a stark reminder for tenants to verify landlord credentials and rental agreements thoroughly. Whilst most landlords operate legitimately, prospective renters might consider checking property ownership details and using protected deposit schemes to reduce fraud risks.

item.title: Opinion: The market is shrinking for some agents, but not the smart ones
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The shifting landscape for estate agents

The property market is undergoing significant changes, with recent data highlighting a notable decline in available rental stock. Many landlords are choosing to sell their properties, driven by increasing legislative pressures, compliance demands, and the introduction of the Renters' Reform Act. Over the past two years, advertised rental stock has fallen by 17%, with more than 254,000 previously rented homes now listed for sale – equating to roughly 700 properties entering the sales market daily.

For estate agents, this trend presents both challenges and opportunities. Those who adapt quickly by offering tailored support to landlords may find themselves better positioned in a competitive market. Landlords, particularly those managing properties independently, often face difficulties keeping up with evolving tenancy laws and safety regulations. Proactive agents who provide guidance on compliance, portfolio management, and long-term strategy could help prevent further exits from the rental market.

How agents can support landlords

With growing uncertainty, landlords may increasingly turn to professionals for assistance. Key areas where agents can add value include:

  • Helping landlords navigate regulatory changes, including the Renters' Reform Act
  • Improving property standards and compliance across portfolios
  • Offering fully managed services to reduce administrative burdens

Agents who engage early with landlords – particularly accidental landlords, those considering selling, or those dealing with problematic tenancies – may build stronger, long-term relationships. Educational content, transparent communication, and tailored solutions could prove crucial in retaining clients and stabilising stock levels.

As the market evolves, agents who take a proactive approach may find themselves better equipped to thrive amidst ongoing changes. The demand for expertise is clear; the question is whether agencies will position themselves as trusted advisors before landlords make alternative decisions.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The shifting landscape for estate agents

The property market is undergoing significant changes, with recent data highlighting a notable decline in available rental stock. Many landlords are choosing to sell their properties, driven by increasing legislative pressures, compliance demands, and the introduction of the Renters' Reform Act. Over the past two years, advertised rental stock has fallen by 17%, with more than 254,000 previously rented homes now listed for sale – equating to roughly 700 properties entering the sales market daily.

For estate agents, this trend presents both challenges and opportunities. Those who adapt quickly by offering tailored support to landlords may find themselves better positioned in a competitive market. Landlords, particularly those managing properties independently, often face difficulties keeping up with evolving tenancy laws and safety regulations. Proactive agents who provide guidance on compliance, portfolio management, and long-term strategy could help prevent further exits from the rental market.

How agents can support landlords

With growing uncertainty, landlords may increasingly turn to professionals for assistance. Key areas where agents can add value include:

  • Helping landlords navigate regulatory changes, including the Renters' Reform Act
  • Improving property standards and compliance across portfolios
  • Offering fully managed services to reduce administrative burdens

Agents who engage early with landlords – particularly accidental landlords, those considering selling, or those dealing with problematic tenancies – may build stronger, long-term relationships. Educational content, transparent communication, and tailored solutions could prove crucial in retaining clients and stabilising stock levels.

As the market evolves, agents who take a proactive approach may find themselves better equipped to thrive amidst ongoing changes. The demand for expertise is clear; the question is whether agencies will position themselves as trusted advisors before landlords make alternative decisions.

item.title: Property industry reacts to latest Rightmove House Price Index
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

New data from Rightmove reveals a notable shift in the UK property market, with average asking prices falling by 0.6% (£2,113) in June – the largest monthly decline for this time of year since 2012. The typical asking price now stands at £376,191, as sellers adjust to increased competition and changing buyer behaviour in a more cautious marketplace.

Market conditions drive price adjustments

Several factors appear to be influencing this unusual June price movement. The property portal highlights greater housing stock availability alongside buyers becoming increasingly price-sensitive. Seasonal patterns may also have shifted, with May's warm weather potentially bringing forward the traditional summer slowdown. Meanwhile, major sporting events like the World Cup could be temporarily diverting attention from property searches.

Industry experts suggest this reflects a market recalibration rather than a loss of confidence. Nathan Emerson of Propertymark notes: "Buyers remain active but are taking more time to make decisions. Realistically priced homes continue to attract strong interest, demonstrating the importance of accurate valuations in the current climate."

Regional variations persist

While national figures show a clear trend, local market conditions continue to vary significantly. Areas with constrained supply reportedly maintain stronger pricing, particularly for well-presented properties. Conversely, locations with higher stock levels – especially flats – are seeing more pronounced price sensitivity among buyers.

Jeremy Leaf, a north London estate agent, observes: "Sellers needing to attract serious buyers are adopting more realistic pricing strategies. Asking prices are increasingly viewed as starting points for negotiation rather than fixed expectations." First-time buyers in particular appear cautious, weighing mortgage costs and living expenses carefully before committing.

For those considering a move, obtaining professional advice tailored to local conditions could prove valuable. Mortgage affordability improvements may further support market activity, though current transaction levels suggest underlying demand remains resilient despite economic pressures.

The coming months may provide clearer indications of whether this pricing adjustment represents a temporary seasonal shift or the beginning of a longer-term trend. Homeowners planning to sell might benefit from researching their local market thoroughly and considering how to position their property competitively in this changing landscape.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

New data from Rightmove reveals a notable shift in the UK property market, with average asking prices falling by 0.6% (£2,113) in June – the largest monthly decline for this time of year since 2012. The typical asking price now stands at £376,191, as sellers adjust to increased competition and changing buyer behaviour in a more cautious marketplace.
