Several factors appear to be influencing this unusual June price movement. The property portal highlights greater housing stock availability alongside buyers becoming increasingly price-sensitive. Seasonal patterns may also have shifted, with May's warm weather potentially bringing forward the traditional summer slowdown. Meanwhile, major sporting events like the World Cup could be temporarily diverting attention from property searches.

Industry experts suggest this reflects a market recalibration rather than a loss of confidence. Nathan Emerson of Propertymark notes: "Buyers remain active but are taking more time to make decisions. Realistically priced homes continue to attract strong interest, demonstrating the importance of accurate valuations in the current climate."

Regional variations persist

While national figures show a clear trend, local market conditions continue to vary significantly. Areas with constrained supply reportedly maintain stronger pricing, particularly for well-presented properties. Conversely, locations with higher stock levels – especially flats – are seeing more pronounced price sensitivity among buyers.

Jeremy Leaf, a north London estate agent, observes: "Sellers needing to attract serious buyers are adopting more realistic pricing strategies. Asking prices are increasingly viewed as starting points for negotiation rather than fixed expectations." First-time buyers in particular appear cautious, weighing mortgage costs and living expenses carefully before committing.

For those considering a move, obtaining professional advice tailored to local conditions could prove valuable. Mortgage affordability improvements may further support market activity, though current transaction levels suggest underlying demand remains resilient despite economic pressures.

The coming months may provide clearer indications of whether this pricing adjustment represents a temporary seasonal shift or the beginning of a longer-term trend. Homeowners planning to sell might benefit from researching their local market thoroughly and considering how to position their property competitively in this changing landscape.

item.title: Stamp duty has had its day, senior MP tells agents
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The Conservative Party has reiterated its proposal to abolish stamp duty on primary residences, arguing the move could revitalise the housing market and accelerate homebuilding. Shadow Chancellor Sir Mel Stride championed the policy during a recent address at Propertymark One, framing it as a solution to Britain’s stagnant property market and chronic housing shortage.

Could scrapping stamp duty boost housing supply?

Conservative modelling suggests eliminating the tax could spur the construction of 200,000 additional homes over five years – a 25% increase on current building rates. The proposal comes as Labour faces scrutiny over its pledge to deliver 1.5 million new homes this parliamentary term, with recent figures showing housing starts lagging behind the required pace.

Sir Mel criticised stamp duty as “a tax on aspiration” that distorts the property market, particularly for first-time buyers. He highlighted how frozen tax thresholds and rising house prices continue to push more properties into higher stamp duty bands, creating what he called “a disaster for both society and the economy”.

The growing stamp duty burden

Analysis shows the tax’s impact has intensified in recent years. The withdrawal of temporary reliefs in 2023 increased costs for many buyers, while fiscal drag – where tax brackets remain static as prices rise – means more homeowners face steeper charges. The Office for Budget Responsibility projects the total stamp duty burden could nearly double by 2030 compared to 2024 levels.

Proponents argue the policy could:

  • Increase housing market transactions
  • Reduce barriers for first-time buyers
  • Provide developers with greater certainty to expand projects

However, critics question whether the Treasury could replace the significant revenue stamp duty generates, which funds essential public services. The debate continues as both major parties grapple with balancing housing market stimulation against fiscal responsibility.

With housing affordability remaining a key concern for voters, the stamp duty discussion looks set to feature prominently in the run-up to the next general election. Homeowners and prospective buyers may wish to monitor how these proposals develop, as changes could significantly impact property purchasing decisions.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The Conservative Party has reiterated its proposal to abolish stamp duty on primary residences, arguing the move could revitalise the housing market and accelerate homebuilding. Shadow Chancellor Sir Mel Stride championed the policy during a recent address at Propertymark One, framing it as a solution to Britain’s stagnant property market and chronic housing shortage.

Could scrapping stamp duty boost housing supply?

Conservative modelling suggests eliminating the tax could spur the construction of 200,000 additional homes over five years – a 25% increase on current building rates. The proposal comes as Labour faces scrutiny over its pledge to deliver 1.5 million new homes this parliamentary term, with recent figures showing housing starts lagging behind the required pace.

Sir Mel criticised stamp duty as “a tax on aspiration” that distorts the property market, particularly for first-time buyers. He highlighted how frozen tax thresholds and rising house prices continue to push more properties into higher stamp duty bands, creating what he called “a disaster for both society and the economy”.

The growing stamp duty burden

Analysis shows the tax’s impact has intensified in recent years. The withdrawal of temporary reliefs in 2023 increased costs for many buyers, while fiscal drag – where tax brackets remain static as prices rise – means more homeowners face steeper charges. The Office for Budget Responsibility projects the total stamp duty burden could nearly double by 2030 compared to 2024 levels.

Proponents argue the policy could:

  • Increase housing market transactions
  • Reduce barriers for first-time buyers
  • Provide developers with greater certainty to expand projects

However, critics question whether the Treasury could replace the significant revenue stamp duty generates, which funds essential public services. The debate continues as both major parties grapple with balancing housing market stimulation against fiscal responsibility.

With housing affordability remaining a key concern for voters, the stamp duty discussion looks set to feature prominently in the run-up to the next general election. Homeowners and prospective buyers may wish to monitor how these proposals develop, as changes could significantly impact property purchasing decisions.

item.title: Fewer rent hikes after Renters’ Rights Act launch, but bigger increases may be coming
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The introduction of the Renters’ Rights Act appears to be reshaping the rental market, with new data revealing fewer tenants facing rent increases, though those rises may be steeper when they occur. Analysis from Hamptons lettings agency shows a 23% drop in tenants experiencing rent hikes in May 2026 compared to the same month last year.

Fewer Increases, Larger Adjustments

If current trends continue, approximately 31% of tenants could see their rent rise this year, down from 40% in 2025 and 50% at the start of 2024. This shift coincides with landlords adapting to new rules limiting rent increases to once annually. However, the average increase for affected tenants in May reached 5.4%, with Scotland – where similar regulations have been in place longer – recording a sharper 7.7% average rise.

The Scottish experience suggests landlords may be opting for fewer but more substantial adjustments. Since 2017, Scotland has operated periodic tenancies with initial 3% annual rent increase caps (lifted in April 2024), leading to market corrections followed by this pattern of larger, less frequent adjustments.

Regional Variations Emerge

Early data indicates regional disparities in how the changes are playing out:

  • London: Outer London saw a 5.3% average rent increase for existing tenants, while Inner London recorded 4.4%
  • South East: The highest regional increase at 5.5% for sitting tenants
  • Midlands: West Midlands led with 6.2% average rises
  • North: Yorkshire & Humber showed more modest increases at 5.4%

As the market continues adapting to the Renters’ Rights Act, tenants might experience fewer rent reviews but potentially face more significant annual adjustments. Landlords and renters alike may wish to monitor these evolving patterns when planning their housing budgets.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The introduction of the Renters’ Rights Act appears to be reshaping the rental market, with new data revealing fewer tenants facing rent increases, though those rises may be steeper when they occur. Analysis from Hamptons lettings agency shows a 23% drop in tenants experiencing rent hikes in May 2026 compared to the same month last year.

Fewer Increases, Larger Adjustments

If current trends continue, approximately 31% of tenants could see their rent rise this year, down from 40% in 2025 and 50% at the start of 2024. This shift coincides with landlords adapting to new rules limiting rent increases to once annually. However, the average increase for affected tenants in May reached 5.4%, with Scotland – where similar regulations have been in place longer – recording a sharper 7.7% average rise.

The Scottish experience suggests landlords may be opting for fewer but more substantial adjustments. Since 2017, Scotland has operated periodic tenancies with initial 3% annual rent increase caps (lifted in April 2024), leading to market corrections followed by this pattern of larger, less frequent adjustments.

Regional Variations Emerge

Early data indicates regional disparities in how the changes are playing out:

  • London: Outer London saw a 5.3% average rent increase for existing tenants, while Inner London recorded 4.4%
  • South East: The highest regional increase at 5.5% for sitting tenants
  • Midlands: West Midlands led with 6.2% average rises
  • North: Yorkshire & Humber showed more modest increases at 5.4%

As the market continues adapting to the Renters’ Rights Act, tenants might experience fewer rent reviews but potentially face more significant annual adjustments. Landlords and renters alike may wish to monitor these evolving patterns when planning their housing budgets.

item.title: Biggest June house price drop in 14 years signals more realistic pricing
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

June has seen the steepest monthly drop in house prices for this time of year since 2012, according to the latest data from Rightmove. The average asking price fell by 0.6%, equivalent to £2,113, bringing the typical price of a newly listed property to £376,191. This decline marks a 0.5% decrease compared to the same period last year, signalling a shift towards more realistic pricing in a competitive market.

Market Dynamics Driving Price Adjustments

A key factor behind the dip is the high volume of properties available for sale, which has created a buyer’s market in many areas. With more homes on the market, sellers are facing increased competition, prompting many to adjust their pricing expectations from the outset. Rightmove highlights that over a third of newly listed properties currently fail to find a buyer, emphasising the importance of setting realistic asking prices to attract interest.

Regional variations are also playing a role. Southern England and Wales have experienced the most noticeable declines, while more affordable areas, such as the North East and Scotland, have shown greater resilience. Despite these adjustments, sales activity remains relatively stable. Buyer demand in May was 10% lower than a year ago but aligns with levels seen throughout 2024. The number of sales agreed dipped by 6% year-on-year, though transaction volumes remain comparable with those of last year.

Summer Slowdown and Mortgage Rate Trends

The market appears to be entering its traditional summer slowdown earlier than usual, with new listings down 5% compared to May 2024. However, stock levels remain elevated, with 6% more homes available for sale than in 2024 and 12% above 2023 levels. This surplus of properties continues to exert downward pressure on prices.

On a positive note, mortgage affordability has improved slightly in recent weeks. Rightmove’s mortgage tracker shows the average two-year fixed rate has decreased from 5.18% to 5.07% over the past month, reducing average monthly repayments by around £30. While this change is modest, it could help improve market sentiment and affordability for prospective buyers.

Colleen Babcock, head of partner marketing at Rightmove, commented: “Overall activity remains within a typical historic range, but buyer behaviour has shifted. With more properties to choose from and higher borrowing costs, buyers are taking longer to make decisions. It’s a very price-sensitive market, and sellers need to ensure their pricing reflects this to attract interest.”

As the market navigates these changes, homeowners may wish to carefully consider their pricing strategies, particularly in areas with high competition. With mortgage rates showing small signs of improvement, the coming months could offer opportunities for buyers and sellers alike.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

June has seen the steepest monthly drop in house prices for this time of year since 2012, according to the latest data from Rightmove. The average asking price fell by 0.6%, equivalent to £2,113, bringing the typical price of a newly listed property to £376,191. This decline marks a 0.5% decrease compared to the same period last year, signalling a shift towards more realistic pricing in a competitive market.

Market Dynamics Driving Price Adjustments

A key factor behind the dip is the high volume of properties available for sale, which has created a buyer’s market in many areas. With more homes on the market, sellers are facing increased competition, prompting many to adjust their pricing expectations from the outset. Rightmove highlights that over a third of newly listed properties currently fail to find a buyer, emphasising the importance of setting realistic asking prices to attract interest.

Regional variations are also playing a role. Southern England and Wales have experienced the most noticeable declines, while more affordable areas, such as the North East and Scotland, have shown greater resilience. Despite these adjustments, sales activity remains relatively stable. Buyer demand in May was 10% lower than a year ago but aligns with levels seen throughout 2024. The number of sales agreed dipped by 6% year-on-year, though transaction volumes remain comparable with those of last year.

Summer Slowdown and Mortgage Rate Trends

The market appears to be entering its traditional summer slowdown earlier than usual, with new listings down 5% compared to May 2024. However, stock levels remain elevated, with 6% more homes available for sale than in 2024 and 12% above 2023 levels. This surplus of properties continues to exert downward pressure on prices.

On a positive note, mortgage affordability has improved slightly in recent weeks. Rightmove’s mortgage tracker shows the average two-year fixed rate has decreased from 5.18% to 5.07% over the past month, reducing average monthly repayments by around £30. While this change is modest, it could help improve market sentiment and affordability for prospective buyers.

Colleen Babcock, head of partner marketing at Rightmove, commented: “Overall activity remains within a typical historic range, but buyer behaviour has shifted. With more properties to choose from and higher borrowing costs, buyers are taking longer to make decisions. It’s a very price-sensitive market, and sellers need to ensure their pricing reflects this to attract interest.”

As the market navigates these changes, homeowners may wish to carefully consider their pricing strategies, particularly in areas with high competition. With mortgage rates showing small signs of improvement, the coming months could offer opportunities for buyers and sellers alike.

item.title: Letting agency handed three-year ban
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

A London letting agency and its directors have been banned from operating in the private rental sector for three years following serious housing violations. The First-tier Tribunal imposed the orders after Tower Hamlets Council uncovered unlawful practices at an unlicensed House in Multiple Occupation (HMO) in Poplar.

Unsafe Conditions and Legal Breaches

R&G Agent Ltd and directors Rafael Mendes Torres and Evandro dos Santos Gomes faced enforcement action after investigations revealed shocking conditions at a converted two-bedroom flat on Kemps Drive. The property, housing up to six tenants, had been illegally transformed into five bedrooms with multiple hazards including:

  • Severe pest infestations including mice and cockroaches
  • Dangerous electrical systems and poor maintenance
  • Missing fire safety measures such as extinguishers and proper doors
  • Failure to protect tenant deposits or provide written agreements

Tenants reported that complaints about the squalid conditions were met with rent increases rather than repairs. The tribunal noted the landlords displayed a "dangerous" lack of understanding of their legal obligations.

Repeat Offenders Face Consequences

This wasn't the company's first offence - they had previous convictions for similar violations at another property. The tribunal found evidence of deliberate attempts to mislead both tenants and council officials about the property's condition and licensing status.

The three-year banning order, effective from 21st August 2024, prevents the company and its directors from:

  • Letting or managing rental properties
  • Working as letting agents
  • Holding any rental licences in England

Tower Hamlets Council has emphasised its commitment to rooting out rogue operators in the private rental sector. As local authorities across the UK intensify enforcement against substandard housing, this case serves as a stark reminder of the consequences for landlords who flout housing regulations.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

A London letting agency and its directors have been banned from operating in the private rental sector for three years following serious housing violations. The First-tier Tribunal imposed the orders after Tower Hamlets Council uncovered unlawful practices at an unlicensed House in Multiple Occupation (HMO) in Poplar.

Unsafe Conditions and Legal Breaches

R&G Agent Ltd and directors Rafael Mendes Torres and Evandro dos Santos Gomes faced enforcement action after investigations revealed shocking conditions at a converted two-bedroom flat on Kemps Drive. The property, housing up to six tenants, had been illegally transformed into five bedrooms with multiple hazards including:

  • Severe pest infestations including mice and cockroaches
  • Dangerous electrical systems and poor maintenance
  • Missing fire safety measures such as extinguishers and proper doors
  • Failure to protect tenant deposits or provide written agreements

Tenants reported that complaints about the squalid conditions were met with rent increases rather than repairs. The tribunal noted the landlords displayed a "dangerous" lack of understanding of their legal obligations.

Repeat Offenders Face Consequences

This wasn't the company's first offence - they had previous convictions for similar violations at another property. The tribunal found evidence of deliberate attempts to mislead both tenants and council officials about the property's condition and licensing status.

The three-year banning order, effective from 21st August 2024, prevents the company and its directors from:

  • Letting or managing rental properties
  • Working as letting agents
  • Holding any rental licences in England

Tower Hamlets Council has emphasised its commitment to rooting out rogue operators in the private rental sector. As local authorities across the UK intensify enforcement against substandard housing, this case serves as a stark reminder of the consequences for landlords who flout housing regulations.

item.title: Pet Requests Under the Renters’ Rights Act: A Cautionary Tale
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Tenants' Rights to Pet Ownership in Rental Properties

A recent case highlighted by LandlordZone has brought attention to tenants' rights regarding pet ownership in rental properties. The situation involved a tenant who successfully challenged her landlord's initial refusal to allow her to keep a cat in her one-bedroom flat, with support from pet advocacy charity AdovCATS.

Under the Renters' Reform Bill, tenants in England now have stronger rights to request permission to keep pets in their rented homes. Landlords cannot unreasonably refuse such requests, though they may impose reasonable conditions. This represents a significant shift in the balance between tenant and landlord rights regarding pet ownership.

What This Means for Renters and Landlords

For tenants considering pet ownership:

  • Requests must be made in writing to the landlord
  • Landlords must respond within 42 days
  • Refusals must be based on valid reasons, such as property suitability

Landlords might consider:

  • Reviewing tenancy agreements to ensure pet clauses are compliant
  • Being prepared to justify any refusals with reasonable arguments
  • Potentially requiring additional pet deposits or specific cleaning clauses

This case serves as an important reminder that the legal landscape surrounding pets in rental properties is evolving. Both tenants and landlords may wish to familiarise themselves with the current regulations to avoid potential disputes. While the law aims to strike a fair balance, individual circumstances will always play a role in these decisions.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Tenants' Rights to Pet Ownership in Rental Properties

A recent case highlighted by LandlordZone has brought attention to tenants' rights regarding pet ownership in rental properties. The situation involved a tenant who successfully challenged her landlord's initial refusal to allow her to keep a cat in her one-bedroom flat, with support from pet advocacy charity AdovCATS.

Under the Renters' Reform Bill, tenants in England now have stronger rights to request permission to keep pets in their rented homes. Landlords cannot unreasonably refuse such requests, though they may impose reasonable conditions. This represents a significant shift in the balance between tenant and landlord rights regarding pet ownership.

What This Means for Renters and Landlords

For tenants considering pet ownership:

  • Requests must be made in writing to the landlord
  • Landlords must respond within 42 days
  • Refusals must be based on valid reasons, such as property suitability

Landlords might consider:

  • Reviewing tenancy agreements to ensure pet clauses are compliant
  • Being prepared to justify any refusals with reasonable arguments
  • Potentially requiring additional pet deposits or specific cleaning clauses

This case serves as an important reminder that the legal landscape surrounding pets in rental properties is evolving. Both tenants and landlords may wish to familiarise themselves with the current regulations to avoid potential disputes. While the law aims to strike a fair balance, individual circumstances will always play a role in these decisions.

item.title: Landlord Law Newsround #440
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Government Rejects Calls for Stricter HMO Regulations

Housing Minister Matthew Pennycook has dismissed petitions requesting additional legislation to curb the conversion of family homes into Houses in Multiple Occupation (HMOs). The government maintains that local councils already possess adequate powers, including Article 4 Directions, to manage HMO concentrations where necessary. Critics argue that rising HMO numbers contribute to parking pressures, anti-social behaviour and strain on local services. Homeowners concerned about HMO proliferation in their area may wish to contact their local planning authority directly.

New Guidance for Landlords on Pet-Friendly Lets

The Dogs Trust has published recommendations for landlords navigating the recent changes under the Renters' Reform Act, which prevents blanket bans on pets. Key considerations include ensuring suitable living conditions for animals, such as adequate space, resting areas and safety measures like baby gates. The guidance notes that multiple pet households require individual assessment, factoring in the property layout and owner's experience. In one reported case, tenant advocacy group Advocat successfully challenged a landlord attempting to impose unreasonable pet-related conditions, highlighting the importance of compliance with the new legislation.

Informal Lets to Friends and Family Carry Risks

A Hiscox survey reveals 63% of landlords have rented to acquaintances, with 18% doing so without formal agreements. Industry experts warn that even lettings to friends or relatives remain subject to full regulatory requirements. Informal arrangements may lead to significant legal and financial consequences, including difficulties regaining possession or facing penalties for non-compliance. Property owners might consider professional advice before entering into any rental agreement, regardless of the tenant's relationship to them.

As the private rental sector continues to evolve, staying informed about legislative changes and best practices remains crucial for all landlords. Those with concerns about specific situations may find it helpful to consult official government guidance or seek independent legal advice.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Government Rejects Calls for Stricter HMO Regulations

Housing Minister Matthew Pennycook has dismissed petitions requesting additional legislation to curb the conversion of family homes into Houses in Multiple Occupation (HMOs). The government maintains that local councils already possess adequate powers, including Article 4 Directions, to manage HMO concentrations where necessary. Critics argue that rising HMO numbers contribute to parking pressures, anti-social behaviour and strain on local services. Homeowners concerned about HMO proliferation in their area may wish to contact their local planning authority directly.

New Guidance for Landlords on Pet-Friendly Lets

The Dogs Trust has published recommendations for landlords navigating the recent changes under the Renters' Reform Act, which prevents blanket bans on pets. Key considerations include ensuring suitable living conditions for animals, such as adequate space, resting areas and safety measures like baby gates. The guidance notes that multiple pet households require individual assessment, factoring in the property layout and owner's experience. In one reported case, tenant advocacy group Advocat successfully challenged a landlord attempting to impose unreasonable pet-related conditions, highlighting the importance of compliance with the new legislation.

Informal Lets to Friends and Family Carry Risks

A Hiscox survey reveals 63% of landlords have rented to acquaintances, with 18% doing so without formal agreements. Industry experts warn that even lettings to friends or relatives remain subject to full regulatory requirements. Informal arrangements may lead to significant legal and financial consequences, including difficulties regaining possession or facing penalties for non-compliance. Property owners might consider professional advice before entering into any rental agreement, regardless of the tenant's relationship to them.

As the private rental sector continues to evolve, staying informed about legislative changes and best practices remains crucial for all landlords. Those with concerns about specific situations may find it helpful to consult official government guidance or seek independent legal advice.

item.title: Why Overseas Property Buyers Need More Than Pretty Listing Photos
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The Importance of Due Diligence for Overseas Property Buyers

Purchasing property abroad presents exciting opportunities but also unique challenges. Whilst attractive listing photos may initially draw buyers in, those investing in overseas real estate often require far more comprehensive information before committing to such a significant financial decision.

Unlike domestic purchases where buyers can easily visit properties in person, overseas investors frequently rely on digital representations alone. This makes thorough due diligence particularly crucial to avoid unexpected issues that might only become apparent during an in-person viewing.

Key Considerations Beyond Property Imagery

Prospective international buyers might consider several important factors that extend beyond appealing photographs:

  • Local market conditions and property valuation trends
  • Legal frameworks and ownership regulations in the target country
  • Currency exchange risks and international payment processes
  • Tax implications both abroad and in the UK
  • Ongoing maintenance and property management options

Virtual tours and detailed video walkthroughs could provide more comprehensive property insights than static images alone. However, even these may not reveal structural issues or neighbourhood characteristics that become apparent during physical inspections.

Mitigating Risks in Cross-Border Transactions

International property purchases inherently carry additional complexities compared to domestic transactions. Buyers may wish to explore professional services that can help navigate these challenges:

  • Independent property valuation services
  • Local legal experts specialising in real estate
  • Reputable international surveyors
  • Currency exchange specialists
  • Bilingual estate agents with cross-border experience

Whilst these services represent additional costs, they might prove valuable in preventing expensive mistakes or uncovering issues that could affect the property's long-term value.

For those considering overseas property investment, comprehensive research and professional guidance could make the difference between a successful international purchase and a problematic transaction. The most attractive listing photos rarely tell the full story, making thorough due diligence essential for cross-border buyers.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The Importance of Due Diligence for Overseas Property Buyers

Purchasing property abroad presents exciting opportunities but also unique challenges. Whilst attractive listing photos may initially draw buyers in, those investing in overseas real estate often require far more comprehensive information before committing to such a significant financial decision.

Unlike domestic purchases where buyers can easily visit properties in person, overseas investors frequently rely on digital representations alone. This makes thorough due diligence particularly crucial to avoid unexpected issues that might only become apparent during an in-person viewing.

Key Considerations Beyond Property Imagery

Prospective international buyers might consider several important factors that extend beyond appealing photographs:

  • Local market conditions and property valuation trends
  • Legal frameworks and ownership regulations in the target country
  • Currency exchange risks and international payment processes
  • Tax implications both abroad and in the UK
  • Ongoing maintenance and property management options

Virtual tours and detailed video walkthroughs could provide more comprehensive property insights than static images alone. However, even these may not reveal structural issues or neighbourhood characteristics that become apparent during physical inspections.

Mitigating Risks in Cross-Border Transactions

International property purchases inherently carry additional complexities compared to domestic transactions. Buyers may wish to explore professional services that can help navigate these challenges:

  • Independent property valuation services
  • Local legal experts specialising in real estate
  • Reputable international surveyors
  • Currency exchange specialists
  • Bilingual estate agents with cross-border experience

Whilst these services represent additional costs, they might prove valuable in preventing expensive mistakes or uncovering issues that could affect the property's long-term value.

For those considering overseas property investment, comprehensive research and professional guidance could make the difference between a successful international purchase and a problematic transaction. The most attractive listing photos rarely tell the full story, making thorough due diligence essential for cross-border buyers.

item.title: Warning signs for UK housing market as property sales slump
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK housing market has shown signs of cooling in early June, with agreed sales falling nearly 9% compared to the same period last year. Recent data reveals regional variations, with London and Northern Ireland experiencing the most pronounced slowdowns, whilst Scotland and Yorkshire & Humber demonstrated relative stability. These figures suggest a loss of momentum following a stronger start to 2026.

Regional variations in market activity

Property sales agreed during the first week of June presented a mixed picture across the UK. Northern Ireland saw the steepest decline at 48.75%, followed by Outer London (18.45%) and Inner London (13.12%). The South West and North East also recorded double-digit decreases in transactions. In contrast, Scotland's market remained virtually unchanged with just a 0.6% dip, and Yorkshire & Humber saw a modest 2.06% reduction.

Whilst the overall trend points to softening demand, the number of new property listings tells a different story. Week 22 of 2026 saw 38,900 new listings – above both the 2026 weekly average (37,400) and the 10-year average for this period (30,900). Year-to-date figures show listings running 0.5% ahead of 2025 and 5.9% higher than 2024 levels.

Contextualising the slowdown

Market analysts note that whilst the year-on-year comparison shows a decline, the figures must be viewed in context. The 2025 data being compared against followed an unusually active spring market, whilst 2026 has seen more typical seasonal patterns. The current weekly average of 24,800 sales agreed remains slightly above the 10-year average of 23,500 for this period.

Several factors could be influencing the current market dynamics:

  • Potential buyers awaiting clearer economic signals
  • Seasonal variations in market activity
  • Regional affordability pressures, particularly in London
  • The impact of recent mortgage rate fluctuations

For homeowners considering selling, the relatively high number of new listings suggests competition may be increasing in some areas. Borrowers might find it valuable to monitor local market conditions and seek independent mortgage advice to understand how these trends could affect their options.

As the summer market develops, industry observers will be watching to see whether this represents a temporary adjustment or the beginning of a more sustained slowdown. The coming weeks' data may provide clearer indications of the market's direction for the remainder of 2026.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK housing market has shown signs of cooling in early June, with agreed sales falling nearly 9% compared to the same period last year. Recent data reveals regional variations, with London and Northern Ireland experiencing the most pronounced slowdowns, whilst Scotland and Yorkshire & Humber demonstrated relative stability. These figures suggest a loss of momentum following a stronger start to 2026.

Regional variations in market activity

Property sales agreed during the first week of June presented a mixed picture across the UK. Northern Ireland saw the steepest decline at 48.75%, followed by Outer London (18.45%) and Inner London (13.12%). The South West and North East also recorded double-digit decreases in transactions. In contrast, Scotland's market remained virtually unchanged with just a 0.6% dip, and Yorkshire & Humber saw a modest 2.06% reduction.

Whilst the overall trend points to softening demand, the number of new property listings tells a different story. Week 22 of 2026 saw 38,900 new listings – above both the 2026 weekly average (37,400) and the 10-year average for this period (30,900). Year-to-date figures show listings running 0.5% ahead of 2025 and 5.9% higher than 2024 levels.

Contextualising the slowdown

Market analysts note that whilst the year-on-year comparison shows a decline, the figures must be viewed in context. The 2025 data being compared against followed an unusually active spring market, whilst 2026 has seen more typical seasonal patterns. The current weekly average of 24,800 sales agreed remains slightly above the 10-year average of 23,500 for this period.

Several factors could be influencing the current market dynamics:

  • Potential buyers awaiting clearer economic signals
  • Seasonal variations in market activity
  • Regional affordability pressures, particularly in London
  • The impact of recent mortgage rate fluctuations

For homeowners considering selling, the relatively high number of new listings suggests competition may be increasing in some areas. Borrowers might find it valuable to monitor local market conditions and seek independent mortgage advice to understand how these trends could affect their options.

As the summer market develops, industry observers will be watching to see whether this represents a temporary adjustment or the beginning of a more sustained slowdown. The coming weeks' data may provide clearer indications of the market's direction for the remainder of 2026.

item.title: Fast-growing property firm swoops for long-established agency
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Dacre, Son & Hartley, a well-established Yorkshire estate agency, has expanded its lettings division with the acquisition of Craven Homelet Services, a Settle-based firm with over three decades of experience in North Yorkshire’s rental market. Founded in 1988, Craven Homelet manages a diverse portfolio of properties across North Craven and Ribblesdale, including the picturesque Yorkshire Dales. This move strengthens Dacre, Son & Hartley’s presence in the region and aligns with its broader growth strategy in the lettings sector.

A Strategic Expansion

This acquisition marks the latest step in Dacre, Son & Hartley’s ambitious growth plans, which include expanding its lettings and property management operations. Earlier this year, the firm relaunched its lettings division, followed by the acquisition of Lister Haigh, a North Yorkshire estate agency and rural consultancy, in 2023. These strategic moves have significantly boosted the number of rental properties under its management, doubling its portfolio over the past 12 months.

Craven Homelet’s owner, Robert Senior, will join Dacre, Son & Hartley as part of the deal, bringing his extensive expertise to lead lettings and property management services in the Settle area. The acquisition also expands the firm’s specialist lettings team to eight members, further enhancing its ability to meet growing landlord and tenant demand.

Building on Local Expertise

Craven Homelet’s long-standing reputation in the local rental market makes it a valuable addition to Dacre, Son & Hartley’s network. With nearly four decades of experience, the firm has managed a wide range of properties across Ribblesdale and the Three Peaks area, earning the trust of landlords and tenants alike. Robert Senior’s deep knowledge of the market and strong relationships within the community are expected to bolster Dacre, Son & Hartley’s commitment to delivering high-quality services.

Patrick McCutcheon, head of residential at Dacre, Son & Hartley, highlighted the significance of the acquisition, stating, “Craven Homelet’s excellent track record and Robert’s expertise make this a natural fit for our business. This move allows us to continue providing consistent, professional support to landlords and tenants while expanding our footprint in the region.”

Meeting Market Challenges

The acquisition comes at a pivotal time for the rental sector, with the recent introduction of the Renters’ Rights Act increasing regulatory and compliance requirements for landlords. As tenant demand remains exceptionally high, many landlords are seeking expert support to navigate these changes. Robert Senior emphasised the importance of this development, noting, “Joining Dacres allows us to leverage their extensive resources and marketing reach, ensuring we can meet the evolving needs of landlords and tenants in this dynamic market.”

With over 200 years of history and a network of 17 offices across North and West Yorkshire, Dacre, Son & Hartley is well-positioned to continue its growth trajectory. This latest acquisition underscores the firm’s commitment to strengthening its lettings division and supporting clients in an increasingly complex rental landscape.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Dacre, Son & Hartley, a well-established Yorkshire estate agency, has expanded its lettings division with the acquisition of Craven Homelet Services, a Settle-based firm with over three decades of experience in North Yorkshire’s rental market. Founded in 1988, Craven Homelet manages a diverse portfolio of properties across North Craven and Ribblesdale, including the picturesque Yorkshire Dales. This move strengthens Dacre, Son & Hartley’s presence in the region and aligns with its broader growth strategy in the lettings sector.

A Strategic Expansion

This acquisition marks the latest step in Dacre, Son & Hartley’s ambitious growth plans, which include expanding its lettings and property management operations. Earlier this year, the firm relaunched its lettings division, followed by the acquisition of Lister Haigh, a North Yorkshire estate agency and rural consultancy, in 2023. These strategic moves have significantly boosted the number of rental properties under its management, doubling its portfolio over the past 12 months.

Craven Homelet’s owner, Robert Senior, will join Dacre, Son & Hartley as part of the deal, bringing his extensive expertise to lead lettings and property management services in the Settle area. The acquisition also expands the firm’s specialist lettings team to eight members, further enhancing its ability to meet growing landlord and tenant demand.

Building on Local Expertise

Craven Homelet’s long-standing reputation in the local rental market makes it a valuable addition to Dacre, Son & Hartley’s network. With nearly four decades of experience, the firm has managed a wide range of properties across Ribblesdale and the Three Peaks area, earning the trust of landlords and tenants alike. Robert Senior’s deep knowledge of the market and strong relationships within the community are expected to bolster Dacre, Son & Hartley’s commitment to delivering high-quality services.

Patrick McCutcheon, head of residential at Dacre, Son & Hartley, highlighted the significance of the acquisition, stating, “Craven Homelet’s excellent track record and Robert’s expertise make this a natural fit for our business. This move allows us to continue providing consistent, professional support to landlords and tenants while expanding our footprint in the region.”

Meeting Market Challenges

The acquisition comes at a pivotal time for the rental sector, with the recent introduction of the Renters’ Rights Act increasing regulatory and compliance requirements for landlords. As tenant demand remains exceptionally high, many landlords are seeking expert support to navigate these changes. Robert Senior emphasised the importance of this development, noting, “Joining Dacres allows us to leverage their extensive resources and marketing reach, ensuring we can meet the evolving needs of landlords and tenants in this dynamic market.”

With over 200 years of history and a network of 17 offices across North and West Yorkshire, Dacre, Son & Hartley is well-positioned to continue its growth trajectory. This latest acquisition underscores the firm’s commitment to strengthening its lettings division and supporting clients in an increasingly complex rental landscape.

item.title: Summer webinar programme to tackle key conveyancing issues
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The Conveyancing Association (CA) has unveiled a new summer webinar initiative designed to help legal professionals navigate pressing challenges in property transactions. Running from June through August, the programme will address operational efficiencies, regulatory updates and emerging business considerations for conveyancing practices.

Key Topics for Property Professionals

The webinar series kicks off on 24th June with a session on automating administrative processes, delivered in collaboration with Post Partner. This will explore streamlining document handling and post allocation systems to reduce workloads. A follow-up session planned for 7th July, developed with Move Reports, will examine stamp duty complexities and new tax registration requirements affecting law firms.

Further instalments throughout the summer will cover:

  • Technological advancements in conveyancing
  • Regulatory compliance updates
  • Client service improvements
  • Professional service innovations

Addressing Industry Changes

Nicky Heathcote, the CA's non-executive chair, highlighted how the initiative taps into affiliate members' expertise to help firms adapt. "The conveyancing sector faces rapid changes - from new regulations to evolving client expectations," she noted. "These webinars aim to equip practices with actionable insights from industry leaders."

The association plans to announce additional webinar dates and topics in coming weeks, with all sessions available exclusively to CA members. Legal professionals looking to stay ahead of sector developments may wish to explore this professional development opportunity as the property market enters its traditionally busier summer period.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The Conveyancing Association (CA) has unveiled a new summer webinar initiative designed to help legal professionals navigate pressing challenges in property transactions. Running from June through August, the programme will address operational efficiencies, regulatory updates and emerging business considerations for conveyancing practices.

Key Topics for Property Professionals

The webinar series kicks off on 24th June with a session on automating administrative processes, delivered in collaboration with Post Partner. This will explore streamlining document handling and post allocation systems to reduce workloads. A follow-up session planned for 7th July, developed with Move Reports, will examine stamp duty complexities and new tax registration requirements affecting law firms.

Further instalments throughout the summer will cover:

  • Technological advancements in conveyancing
  • Regulatory compliance updates
  • Client service improvements
  • Professional service innovations

Addressing Industry Changes

Nicky Heathcote, the CA's non-executive chair, highlighted how the initiative taps into affiliate members' expertise to help firms adapt. "The conveyancing sector faces rapid changes - from new regulations to evolving client expectations," she noted. "These webinars aim to equip practices with actionable insights from industry leaders."

The association plans to announce additional webinar dates and topics in coming weeks, with all sessions available exclusively to CA members. Legal professionals looking to stay ahead of sector developments may wish to explore this professional development opportunity as the property market enters its traditionally busier summer period.
